evidence: README describes contract-governance enforcement as including `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go` under contract-surface CI gating.

The runtime contract gate trigger and guard only treat `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` as contract-surface changes for mandatory test pairing.

Contract compatibility metadata is centralized in separate files (`goseg/protocol/contracts/contract_metadata.go`) that affect lifecycle policy and can drift from documented governance if not part of the same enforced change boundary.

`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` and `goseg/importer/importer.go` rely on large runtime structs composed mostly of function fields, indicating an abstraction-by-indirection pattern rather than behavior-based interfaces.
Runtime construction in those files uses extensive defaulted callback wiring and then forwards through narrow wrappers, creating brittle seams and coupling tests/clients to internal callback names.
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` and `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go` follow the same seam style, including duplicated API/implementation split with explicit passthrough defaults.

Multiple orchestration-related files (`goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_health.go`) define overlapping adapter-style runtimes for related concerns instead of a shared abstraction.
In `.github/workflows/upload-contract-gate.yml`, contract-gate trigger paths do not include `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go` or `goseg/protocol/contracts/contract_metadata.go`.
In `.github/scripts/check-upload-contract.sh`, the gate only flags contract test requirements when `protocol_contracts.go` or `startram_contracts.go` change.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/contract_metadata.go` contains `IntroducedIn/Compatibility` for each contract ID, so lifecycle-policy changes can avoid gate execution and still affect compatibility behavior.

`emitWorkflowTransition` assigns `resolvedRuntime, _ := resolveWorkflowRuntime(runtime)` and continues with `runtime.TransitionEmitter(...)` even when resolution fails, so transition emission side effects are attempted after a potentially invalid runtime resolution.

`publishImportTransitionWithPolicy` resolves runtime via `resolveImporterRuntime(runtime...)` and invokes `PublishImportTransitionFn` without any explicit resolution/requirement validation or early return on runtime misconfiguration.

These helpers produce inconsistent failure contracts: one silently discards validation outcomes, another omits validation entirely, so runtime setup faults are surfaced differently across related transition paths.

findings.summary: Protocol action tokens are derived from ContractID strings, tightly coupling identity format to action binding semantics.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` derives `ActionToken` via `deriveActionToken(id ContractID)` using `strings.Split(string(id), ".")` and taking the last segment.

The same file also builds `contractCatalogFamilySpecs` entries with `Action` derived from that parsing rather than explicit action token constants, while `startram_contracts.go` follows a separate declaration style, increasing family-level coupling risk in the contract registry boundary.
findings.suggestion: Define explicit `ActionToken` constants per protocol action contract (e.g., `ActionUploadOpenEndpointToken = "open-endpoint"`, `ActionUploadResetToken = "reset"`, etc.) and map each `Action` field directly to those constants. Keep `ContractID` as identity and add validation tests ensuring ID suffix can be validated but not used as the source of truth.

findings.summary: Service/runtime abstraction is implemented as callback bags and name dispatch rather than cohesive interfaces, creating fragile coupling across startup and import flows.
evidence: `startuporchestrator` defines a runtime with many callback fields per service concern (start/stop/reload hooks) and routes execution through explicit name-based switch logic.
`importer` runtime struct similarly groups many unrelated function callbacks and forces constructor callers to populate broad option bags even when only one seam is needed.
The same callback-heavy pattern appears in `shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, showing a repeated design choice rather than a one-off local decision.
findings.suggestion: Replace callback-per-method registries with small, composable interfaces per domain (`ServiceRuntime`, `ImporterOperation`, etc.) and a registry/map of typed handlers. Dispatch should be on typed operation objects or enums, not arbitrary function-parameter bags and string switch blocks.

findings.summary: Broadcast state machine uses extra contract/wrapper layers that add indirection without increasing substitution power.
evidence: `state.go` introduces contract/runtime structs and method wrappers that forward calls instead of encapsulating behavior directly.
`loop.go` and `transitions.go` consume these wrappers, increasing the call depth without clear policy/implementation separation.
The observed pass-through style appears to mirror test seams rather than needed abstraction boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Collapse the pass-through contracts where they do not enforce alternate implementations. Keep only one runtime abstraction with explicit, minimal methods (`next`, `handle`, `collect`) and remove redundant wrapper types.

findings.summary: Docker wireguard/subsystem modules duplicate similar adapter abstractions instead of sharing a common orchestration boundary.
evidence: Each file defines separate runtime contracts and function-seam layers for closely related docker orchestration concerns.
There is overlapping plumbing for operation dispatch and test substitution patterns across these files rather than a unifying abstraction for docker subprocess operations.
This increases duplication and makes cross-module changes error-prone as each module evolves independently.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared orchestration contracts package for common docker operations (lifecycle, events, state), then let each subsystem compose this interface rather than defining parallel runtime wrappers.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi runtime indirection is fragmented across API/method files with default seam variables, increasing hidden coupling and reducing clarity.
evidence: Wi-Fi logic is split into `wifi_runtime_api.go` and `wifi_runtime_methods.go` where defaults/seams are exposed as shared runtime call-throughs.
Callers receive abstracted runtime values while concrete behavior is still largely proxied through wrapper methods and default function variables.
Behavior substitution is thus dependent on implicit runtime globals rather than explicit constructor-injected dependencies.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to explicit dependency injection of a small `WiFiRuntime` interface implemented by one concrete type, and remove global/default function-variable seams where possible so tests inject typed fakes directly.

findings.summary: Runtime contract governance scope in CI does not include all contract metadata surfaces referenced by documentation.
evidence: README states contract-gate governance for contract surface changes includes `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go` and related runtime contract surfaces.
The gate configuration paths only monitor `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` and ignore central metadata definition files that govern compatibility policy.
findings.suggestion: Align contract-gate scope with governance docs by adding the shared contract metadata source files (including `contracts.go`/`contract_metadata.go`) to CI path triggers and ensuring the same contract scope matrix includes compatibility-policy ownership files.

findings.summary: Lifecycle metadata-only edits can skip contract gate enforcement and targeted coverage.
evidence: Gate script currently checks contract-surface changes for `protocol_contracts.go`/`startram_contracts.go` only.
Compatibility metadata entries (`IntroducedIn`, `Compatibility`) live in a separate table that influences contract behavior and are not included in the same change-detection branch.
When only metadata changes, no contract test coupling is enforced even though README frames those changes as part of contract surface governance.
findings.suggestion: Treat metadata compatibility changes as first-class contract surface changes: add file-level triggers and script conditions so edits to metadata maps invoke `goseg/protocol/contracts` contract tests, then gate on a mandatory compatibility-policy assertion set for metadata-only diffs.

findings.summary: Workflow transition emitter ignores runtime validation errors before publishing transitions.
evidence: `emitWorkflowTransition` does `resolvedRuntime, _ := resolveWorkflowRuntime(runtime)` and does not check the returned error before calling `resolvedRuntime.TransitionEmitter(...)`.
In a transition-heavy subsystem, this is the only place the emission helper executes, so a misconfigured runtime can continue to a later publish call with inconsistent error behavior.
findings.suggestion: Change this helper to propagate runtime-resolution errors (return wrapped error) and only call the emitter when runtime validation succeeds; if callers need best-effort behavior, make it explicit via a separate non-critical helper with documented semantics.

findings.summary: Importer transition publisher skips runtime validation and calls transition callback without checking setup failure.
evidence: `publishImportTransitionWithPolicy` calls `resolveImporterRuntime(runtime...)` and immediately dereferences `resolvedRuntime.PublishImportTransitionFn` without handling a resolution/validation error path.

Nearby importer workflows rely on validated runtime resolvers in other paths (for example, `resolveImporterRuntimeForUpload`/`resolveImporterRuntimeForWorkflow`), creating two different error contracts for transition publication.
This inconsistency appears alongside transition emission in workflow code, making failure behavior diverge across related lifecycle paths.
findings.suggestion: Resolve transition publisher runtime through a validation-bearing path (e.g., a dedicated `resolve...ForPublish` that enforces required callback contracts) and return an explicit error before attempting `PublishImportTransitionFn`.
